channel_name: This Week in Startups · Jason Calacanis
video_title: An AI that watches your every click may be the future of work | E2314
gist: Brandon Dixon, co-founder/CTO of intent-aware endpoint security startup INT (int.ai, ex-Microsoft founders), pitches an on-device AI agent that reasons about user and agent behavior in real time to stop policy violations before they happen; the company just left stealth with a $100M round targeting Global 2000/Fortune 500. A second segment features David Im of Sume Labs, building an orchestration/router layer over multiple video-gen models to one-shot production-ready marketing videos.

insights.claim: INT (int.ai) has built an on-device AI agent for endpoints (laptops, phones) that assesses in real time whether a user is about to violate corporate policy or do something risky, and stops the action before it occurs.
insights.speaker: Brandon Dixon, co-founder/CTO of INT
insights.so_what: A prevention-at-the-endpoint approach is a category shift from reactive detection; watch whether it can displace incumbent EDR budget.
insights.quote: we wanted to meet them in that moment, look at the work that was taking place and then make an assessment as to whether or not they were going to violate corporate policy or do something they shouldn't and effectively stop that from happening

insights.claim: Dixon argues the security industry has 'largely given up' on prevention and become reactive—waiting for breaches then troubleshooting—and INT's whole thesis is to restore prevention using new reasoning/embedding models at the point of work.
insights.speaker: Brandon Dixon, co-founder/CTO of INT
insights.so_what: Frames a contrarian positioning against the detection-and-response consensus that dominates security spend.
insights.quote: we've largely given up uh prevention inside of security, right? We're very reactive. We wait for the bad thing to occur

insights.claim: Most corporate breaches come from well-intentioned employees making mistakes—clicking things, emailing financial data to the wrong person, sharing credentials across chat—not primarily from sophisticated adversaries.
insights.speaker: Brandon Dixon, co-founder/CTO of INT
insights.so_what: If human error is the dominant breach vector, tools that intercept benign mistakes address a larger surface than adversary-focused defenses.
insights.quote: a lot of the breaches occur because people are wellincclined trying to do their jobs, but they make a mistake

insights.claim: AI adoption creates a new risk surface: non-technical 'citizen developers' pushed by leadership to solve problems with AI can delete system artifacts, pull sensitive context into models, and accidentally leak it outside the company.
insights.speaker: Brandon Dixon, co-founder/CTO of INT
insights.so_what: Enterprise AI rollouts expand the attack/error surface, creating demand for behavioral guardrails around AI usage.
insights.quote: ones that don't have a technical background who are being encouraged by their leadership to you know solve problems with AI

insights.claim: Developers inside enterprises may run ~20 different AI agents simultaneously across roles, multiplying the same leakage and mistake risks at machine scale.
insights.speaker: Brandon Dixon, co-founder/CTO of INT
insights.so_what: Agent proliferation per employee signals a fast-growing need to monitor agent (not just human) behavior.
insights.quote: they might have like 20 different agents running to go and perform uh various roles across the enterprise

insights.claim: INT relies on optimized embedding models rather than heavy reasoning models, letting it make sub-second decisions on CPUs without GPUs at the endpoint—the performance property that makes real-time prevention feasible.
insights.speaker: Brandon Dixon, co-founder/CTO of INT
insights.so_what: CPU-only sub-second inference lowers deployment cost and latency, a technical moat for real-time endpoint intervention.
insights.quote: Embedding models are great because they're very performant. They don't require like GPUs. They can operate on CPUs and when it comes to decision-m they can make decisions in sub-second time frames

insights.claim: INT built a single architecture that runs identically on the endpoint or entirely on the back end, so customers with regulatory constraints can choose where compute happens without a separate product.
insights.speaker: Brandon Dixon, co-founder/CTO of INT
insights.so_what: One deployable architecture across edge and cloud reduces engineering fragmentation and widens the addressable regulated-customer base.
insights.quote: it can run completely on the back end or it can run on the endpoint itself but it's the same architecture

insights.claim: The advance that makes INT possible today versus a couple of years ago is embeddings—the ability to translate semantically meaningful words into high-dimensional numeric representations computers can act on.
insights.speaker: Brandon Dixon, co-founder/CTO of INT
insights.so_what: Ties the product's timing directly to the LLM/embedding wave—an enabling-technology inflection for behavioral security.
insights.quote: the advantage that we have in in things like embeddings the ability to take semantic words like things that we understand have meaning and translate them into something that a computer can understand

insights.claim: INT uses published open models plus optional cloud-provider or customer-brought models for reasoning, but controls and self-optimizes its own embedding models to run performantly on either edge or backend.
insights.speaker: Brandon Dixon, co-founder/CTO of INT
insights.so_what: Owning the embedding layer while renting frontier reasoning keeps costs down and controls the core IP.

insights.claim: With just a corporate policy plus a list of sanctioned software, INT can surface policy violations—like unsanctioned AI, remote-access, or other tools—within 'hour one' of deployment.
insights.speaker: Brandon Dixon, co-founder/CTO of INT
insights.so_what: Fast time-to-value with minimal configuration lowers the barrier to enterprise pilots.
insights.quote: if I understand what like the allowed software is I can immediately tell you people that are using other software be it AI remote access or something else that is not sanctioned for them

insights.claim: INT deploys in a baseline/observability mode first, using a roughly two-week burn-in to establish what is normal per user, per department, and per cohort before it starts intervening—avoiding annoying users with premature prompts.
insights.speaker: Brandon Dixon, co-founder/CTO of INT
insights.so_what: A learn-then-enforce rollout reduces false-positive friction, a common failure mode for behavioral security tools.
insights.quote: there's typically a burn-in period where you might have two weeks to establish baselines of what's normal

insights.claim: Dixon frames INT as building an 'organization work model,' analogous to the world models that made self-driving cars predictive instead of overfit to hand-coded rules—shifting security from static rules to anticipatory behavioral understanding.
insights.speaker: Brandon Dixon, co-founder/CTO of INT
insights.so_what: The world-model framing hints at a far larger 'work observability' platform ambition beyond security.
insights.quote: world models were like super advantageous to cars, right? Because prior to a world model existing that encapsulated the environment, we were trying to tell the car like when to stay in the lanes

insights.claim: Dixon positions the accumulated behavioral context as a 'semantic substrate for security' with broad applicability: closing benign true-positive SOC tickets, adding context to DLP events, surfacing insider risk, and identifying employees who need training.
insights.speaker: Brandon Dixon, co-founder/CTO of INT
insights.so_what: A single behavioral data layer that spans SOC, DLP, and insider-risk use cases implies a multi-product land-and-expand roadmap.
insights.quote: One of the ones that I was playing around with was like the semantic substrate for security

insights.claim: Beyond security there is a productivity angle: understanding what employees do all day lets a company identify mundane, monotonous, or risky work that AI agents could take over, then keep those agents 'on the rails.'
insights.speaker: Brandon Dixon, co-founder/CTO of INT
insights.so_what: The dataset becomes a map for automating work—potentially far more valuable than the security use case alone.
insights.quote: there's a productivity angle to this as well in which if I understand what people are doing throughout the day, then it becomes ripe to figure out what things might agents benefit

insights.claim: Host Alex argues the behavioral dataset INT accumulates would be so valuable that AI labs, private equity, or working groups will try to acquire the company for the data itself.
insights.speaker: host (Alex)
insights.so_what: Frames the underlying work-behavior data as a strategic asset that could drive acquisition interest.
insights.quote: I'm terrified that one of the you know AI lab JV FTE private equity working groups are going to try to scoop you up and then take all the data you have

insights.claim: INT is deployed entirely within the customer's own cloud boundary and INT itself cannot see the data, because Global 2000 buyers demand to own their data and keep IP from going to third-party supply chains.
insights.speaker: Brandon Dixon, co-founder/CTO of INT
insights.so_what: Data-residency-first design is a deliberate wedge into regulated large enterprises wary of sending IP to vendors.
insights.quote: Ant is deployed within the customer's boundary like we don't even get to see it

insights.claim: INT offers granular collection controls—per-toggle data collection, user and endpoint groups, plus role-based and attribute-based access control—so, e.g., developers can be monitored heavily while legal collects almost nothing and SOC analysts are blocked from seeing investigation screenshots.
insights.speaker: Brandon Dixon, co-founder/CTO of INT
insights.so_what: Configurable, permissioned collection is the guardrail that lets granular monitoring pass enterprise privacy review.
insights.quote: for everything that we collect, there's toggles that basically allow the business to turn it on and off

insights.claim: Employee acceptance of granular work monitoring is unsettled—Dixon calls norms 'TBD' and Alex cites Meta's unpopular push to scrutinize what its engineers were doing as a cautionary signal.
insights.speaker: Brandon Dixon and host (Alex)
insights.so_what: Workforce backlash is the key adoption risk for granular behavioral monitoring products.

insights.claim: Dixon argues corporate policy 'lacks teeth' because it is written in natural language and left to employee interpretation, and INT's role is to turn that policy into an actual enforceable control point at the endpoint.
insights.speaker: Brandon Dixon, co-founder/CTO of INT
insights.so_what: Reframes the product as the enforcement layer that makes written policy operational—an ROI story for compliance buyers.
insights.quote: part of the problem with like a corporate policy is that it it lacks teeth. It's only as good as its ability to put it into a control point

insights.claim: INT emerged from stealth with a $100M round and general availability; Dixon justifies the raise by the cost of building a cross-platform endpoint agent, putting AI at the edge, supporting AWS/Google/Azure self-hosting, and landing large enterprise accounts.
insights.speaker: Brandon Dixon, co-founder/CTO of INT
insights.so_what: A $100M seed-scale raise signals investor conviction that behavioral endpoint AI is a large, capital-intensive market.
insights.quote: you announced a $100 million round

insights.claim: Dixon says INT deliberately does not compete head-on with EDR—he calls EDR a commodity everyone already has—and instead augments the gaps EDR, DLP, and insider-risk tools leave, positioning INT as a 'programmable endpoint.'
insights.speaker: Brandon Dixon, co-founder/CTO of INT
insights.so_what: Avoiding the crowded EDR fight and selling augmentation eases the enterprise buy versus rip-and-replace.
insights.quote: From my perspective, EDR is a commodity at this point. Everybody's got something in place

insights.claim: INT offers both a hosted SaaS (dedicated tenant) and one-click self-hosted deployment inside the customer's cloud, but is not open source; when the customer self-hosts, hosting cost shifts to their COGS while licensing remains.
insights.speaker: Brandon Dixon, co-founder/CTO of INT
insights.so_what: A licensing model decoupled from hosting gives predictable pricing and flexibility without an open-source strategy.

insights.claim: Dixon reports strong demand from goodwill built over multiple prior exits: he has hired over 75 people with zero recruiting fees and retained a healthy pipeline of interested buyers even before formally leaving stealth.
insights.speaker: Brandon Dixon, co-founder/CTO of INT
insights.so_what: Founder reputation converting into free hiring and warm pipeline is a de-risking signal for the raise.
insights.quote: I've hired well over, you know, 75 people now. Uh and I've not paid a single recruiting fee

insights.claim: INT targets Global 2000 and above—Fortune 500-scale enterprises—and is currently building out sales reps, sales engineering, and forward-deployed engineering to handle post-stealth inbound.
insights.speaker: Brandon Dixon, co-founder/CTO of INT
insights.so_what: An enterprise-only motion means long sales cycles and heavy GTM spend, informing how to read future ARR milestones.
insights.quote: we go and target big enterprises our our environments are global 2000 and above. So think Fortune 500

insights.claim: David Im's Sume Labs (sume.com) is building an agent-orchestration/router layer wrapping five or six video, image, and audio models to produce a production-ready result and eventually one-shot full marketing videos.
insights.speaker: David Im, founder of Sume Labs
insights.so_what: A model-agnostic orchestration layer is a bet that value moves to routing/reliability above commoditized generative models.
insights.quote: what we're trying to do is make an API that is basically unwrapper of like let's say five or six models to get the production ready result

insights.claim: Im frames current AI video generation as a 'slot machine'—stochastic and unpredictable, requiring repeated prompting where each attempt takes ~30 seconds—and argues removing that randomness raises utilization.
insights.speaker: David Im, founder of Sume Labs
insights.so_what: Reliability/predictability, not raw quality, is the wedge Im is betting monetizes in generative video.
insights.quote: the slot machine is not fun when you're making videos and images and they take 30 seconds. It's incredibly frustrating

insights.claim: Individual video-generation models cap at roughly 15 seconds (about 30 for some), so Sume routes across models and stitches clips with one consistent audio track to produce up to 60 seconds of consistent video.
insights.speaker: David Im, founder of Sume Labs
insights.so_what: Length and consistency limits of base models create the technical gap an orchestration layer monetizes.
insights.quote: video generation models only like support up to 15 seconds it's like 30 seconds for our like CDS like next model

insights.claim: Facial consistency is a core unsolved problem in AI video—a generated person's face often looks right for the first ~7 seconds then distorts toward the end—and maintaining it throughout is a key differentiator Sume targets.
insights.speaker: David Im and host (Alex)
insights.so_what: Whoever solves temporal face consistency unlocks longer, usable AI marketing videos—a concrete technical moat.
insights.quote: for the first like seven seconds of the video it'll look like the person's face and then it sometimes gets like distorted towards the end

insights.claim: Sume has ~20K users and roughly 90% of its paid customers are brands, with its core audience on Instagram and TikTok rather than Twitter; users just pick an avatar and write a script, and each scene is generated three times for selection.
insights.speaker: David Im, founder of Sume Labs
insights.so_what: Brand-heavy paid mix and a simple avatar+script UX indicate a UGC-ad product-market fit signal to track.
insights.quote: right now we have 20K users and the main customers espe especially about 90% of the paid customers are brands
